---
title: "Unit 7 In Class Discussion"
author:
  - Elizabeth King
  - Kevin Middleton
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    standalone: true
    self-contained: true
    logo: QMLS_Logo.png
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
code-annotations: hover
bibliography: QMLS_Bibliography.bib
csl: evolution.csl
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)

theme_set(theme_cowplot(font_size = 20))

```


## What are confidence intervals really?

> I would appreciate going over quiz 1 - I got a little tripped up on some of the questions


## Questions confidence intervals can't answer

- The *probability* that the true mean is greater than 0 is at least 95%.
- The *probability* that the true mean equals 0 is smaller than 5%
- There is a 95% *probability* that the true mean lies between 0.1 and 0.4.
- The "null *hypothesis*" that the true mean equals 0 is likely to be incorrect.


## Question confidence intervals can answer

- If we were to *repeat* the *experiment* over and over, then in 95% of the trials, the confidence intervals *will contain the true mean*.


## Calculating confidence intervals

Quiz 7-3


## Understanding decision criteria

> I know you are giving us examples of how the theory behind the test works, so is the code on slide 10 of lecture 7.3 something we would use to try to see if our mean is something generated by sampling error/chance or would you just calculate a p-value from the t-test to figure that out?


## One-sided vs. two-sided tests

> I'm still a little confused as to when we should choose a One Sided Test versus a Two Sided Test.


## What is Nuzzo talking about?

> When reading through the P value article following lecture 7-3, the authors said, "According to one widely used calculation to an external site., a P value of 0.01 corresponds to a false-alarm probability of at least 11%, depending on the underlying probability that there is a true effect; a P value of 0.05 raises that chance to at least 29%." What calculation is this? Can we talk about this more in depth because this went right over my head.


## What is Nuzzo talking about?

![](https://media.springernature.com/lw703/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2F506150a/MediaObjects/41586_2014_BF506150a_Figb_HTML.jpg)


## Unexpected research questions

> If something initially not part of an experiment becomes of interest through observations during data collection, is there a non biased way to begin collecting and working with data on that trait? Also what can be done to make data more replicable?


## Reviewing `for` loops
